titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, July 12, 1842
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Wake Forest University, University of Tennessee, Xavier University, Texas A&M University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date:

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth, ,

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
How shocked you must have been my dearest mother by the death of our friend Les- lie Foster - You heard it from the newspapers I suppose I heard it from Charles Fox at the moment of my arrival here. I never was more sur prised and shocked ! There is no mention in the papers of his son except that he went up to Dublin next day --! Poor young man He really was fond of his father - It must have been dreadful for him - Charles tells me that Mrs Foster could not believe that her husband was really dead - you She had always ima gined that her son who was drowned might have been recovered if more pains had been taken and upon this recollection she persisted in believing that her husband might be recovered and she wanted to send or to take Marsh down to Cavan immediately But he would not go: he said here are two phy sicians there who have tried to bleed him and tried all that could be tried for his recovery - And in short he would not go and he was right - And there is an end of that old friend - that ambitious, affectionate man -
- And the world goes on - And the question is who comes next into his place - And so it must be - We must either live or die - Happy I think to die at once & with only half an hours suffering to self - much the happiest - But the shock to friends is great -

But now for the living - We had a very pleasant journey here literally in no time - for time was annihilated by Harriets reading some of an old or new book I know not wht of Bannims, An admirable description of a benevolent Catc . Priest who robs him self and his housekeeper of their blankets one night for a wretched family and is told of to his supreme housekeeper by a spy of a gosvon who has been lurking in the kitchen chimney corner & has been direct ly to housekeeper who is out unknownst at her tea drinking & had left him locked in to watch - The final battle between priest & his housekeeper is admirable & I wish that Fanny and you & Lucy could see it

But you are thinking of my teeth - All that were to be pulled out viz two are out and gave me very little pain - Mr . Brophy, tell Lucy is a very different sort of person from what I had expected both in external appearance & speech I had imagined that he was unkempt unwashed round shouldered slump of a thorough going, jocose, tender brougeoneer but I saw before me a jemmy , slim, upright little well accoutred man with nice curled hair and face more Italian & penetrating than broad Irish - and except - "Dear!" and theres a deer " - And wash your mouth again again now like a deer. - " There was none of the persuasive darling wheedling which I expected - I asked for but have not
yet seen the brother - nor the dog 61 - but will certainly see both this morn ing - We are to be with him by 10 o clock - It is now 1/2 past 8) - He has taken a mould of the upper jaw but cannot of the lower till the gums harden where the teeth were - He says he can make as good a job for me as he has done for Lucy - I gave him great pleasure by telling him how well he has succeeded for her and how very comfortable she has been and how thankful she is to him
Harriet has told you all about Mary Anne's teeth - Happy creatures those two girls are - enjoying everything so much - We go back to dear Trim today - 12 o clock - set out -
Your packet has this instant ar- rived. I am very glad you approve of the Margaret Ruxton scheme - And But as Fanny desires I will not decide in a hurry -

Thank you for sending the dress- ing gown - I begin to believe that the thin one is my own after all I suppose you are now in the midst of the greatest to whurry= go nimble about the packing up and the new cook and the house maid who has not arrived & all all upon your shoulders!
div.closer: Gold help you & God bless you. I must go to breakfast and am in all circumstances, even before break fast ever affectionately, your old Maria E

postscript.p: Dear Pakenham my love to him

I return here on Monday - Charles
goes tomorrow - I dont know where

But ultimately or penultimately he goes to Wiesbaden - too near Mayence where the banished Foxs are - He says that if he went another way he should fall into the litter of the other Foxes a little worse - He looks sadly Dear good creature

I hope Francis will be satisfied with Mary Moffatts proposal - I will see her brother at Trim & get him to sign an agreement that I will draw up for swearing the £5pr annm rent & I will send it in to FBE and then she may be let in to the house I think But not till then - And so much the better for Gahan - He will not go into the newly plastered till quite dry -
